author: @marvindebot

replies.author: @commie4164
replies.text:
@CoaxialCrusader razor sharp is about as sharp as most people can get. the kind of sharpness your talking about requires either a machine or skilled labor to achieve, in addition to needing higher end blade steel.

the best knife sharpener in the world wouldnt be able to put that kind of edge on your average machete because the steel in them is cheap and too soft

replies.author: @cavalieroutdoors6036
replies.text: @CoaxialCrusader  maybe if you're talking about the cheap disposable 'razors' with the replaceable blades are used for cutting boxes. But if you ever used a straight razor you'd have a whole new perspective. Razors are the apex of sharp, they have to be in order to shave comfortably. Paradoxically enough, if a blade is too dull it makes for an uncomfortable shave. Even your specialty knives aren't approaching the level of sharp my *dullest* razor is at.

replies.author: @commie4164
replies.text:
@CoaxialCrusader go get a cheap stainless steel butter knife and try to put the kind of edge your talking about on it. it wont happen.

edges like that require an extremely fine surface on a microscopically thin cutting edge. cheap soft steels lack the structure to even get to that point. most cheap steels top out at around the level of a box cutter blade, which is NOT razor sharp by the way

replies.author: @commie4164
replies.text: @CoaxialCrusader strong rebuttal. very convincing. lol

author: @jeannaimarre3537
text:
There's also an elephant in the room rarely adressed in terms of self-defense.
Sure, you could probably knockout an average Joe if you're properly trained, but I've heard enough horror stories from police officer friends who had to be 4 to put down a junkie, and even then they struggled.
Certain substances can alter your physiology and mind so much that you ignore fear and pain, even a 40 kilos hepatitis-riddled junkie or mentally unstable person could kill you, even if you're a pro of close-combat.

replies.author: @tigon8224
replies.text: Cops mostly don’t train grappling and legally can’t use blood chokes which would put a swift end to the conflict and when applied properly are safe

replies.author: @boccobadz
replies.text: There is the difference between fighting to survive, figthing to protect and fighting to disarm. Only the first one works - I'm quite confident in taking on 2 or 3 opponents when I'm alone (no guns involved, even if they have too,s), but if I'm with a girl, we're running away as fast as possible.

replies.author: @Marcus_Postma
replies.text: Blood chokes and grappling don't always work either. I'm a big dude and trained in multiple forms of combat (armed and unarmed) through years of military service. During the higher forms of combative training only the instructor was actually able to apply a blood choke to me because my neck and shoulders are very large, and when it came to grappling I was almost always able to brute force it and simply pick up anyone trying to hold me, and then slam them. With adrenaline coursing through your veins you don't even notice anything until after it's finished.

replies.author: @jeannaimarre3537
replies.text:
​@boccobadzagain, drugs and alcohol can act as force multipliers, in the sense that they dull or suppress pain, alter self-preservation and make the aggressor unpredictable for someone conventionally trained, however well. It's the same problem with mentally disturbed people, with the added risk that unlike with substances, it's often hard to impossible to guess at first glance, which can reduce your vigilance.
Even admitting that you could take care of one or multiple agressors, the health risk remains intact, given that firstly, it's not always obvious, and secondly, unless you wear a hazmat suit at all times contamination via fluid transfer is hard to control in the mess of the fight.
Like I was saying, you might manage 2 - 3 "normal" guys or career criminals, but unless you're able to read minds, it's very difficult to differentiate when time isn't a luxury you have. Not to mention that career criminals can be under the influence of something, or just unstable mentally.

replies.author: @Orquet-qj2nf
replies.text: Or a seventy year old Alzheimer's patient.

author: @davidwhidden9337
text:
A substitute teacher once gave us advice. He trained in MMA and told us teenagers something like this, 

"If I am approached by a guy with a knife, I'm taking out my wallet and handing it to him. I may have formal training, but with that knife he has extra reach. If he was within my range and the situation called for it, I could defend myself, but that would be rare. If I mess up, I'm hurt or worse. It's just money or things that can be replaced." 

A teacher once told us, in a fight it's all about adrenaline. Whomever runs out first loses. It's better not to fight at all. 

The best advice has always been, try nor to be there or be ready to run.

author: @Championwbs
text:
Generally there are 3 rules for self defense that can always be followed:
Rule 1. The best defense is to avoid the fight.
Rule 2. If you can’t avoid the fight do whatever you have to to get away.
Rule 3. Treat everything as if it is deadly. 
You are never in a good situation in a fight, but following these 3 rules generally improves your chances of walking away. Beyond this it depends entirely on the situation, and a number of factors outside of your control. This is the context that is needed for most self defense.

replies.author: @Marinealver
replies.text:
Just like an Active Shooter; your options are to Hide, Run, Fight.
Avoid the fight all together, if that fails then do what ever you can to deescalate, if that fails then DON'T LOSE!

author: @PeregrinPursuivant
text: Sharpness reduces the force needed to pierce flesh. With a scalpel a surgeon just needs slight pressure to cut through skin.

author: @jeffreybarton1297
text:
This subjective idea of sharpness was highlighted a few days ago for me. I was offered a free short sword by a relative. It turned out to be a wall hanger, Mall Ninja type thing. I went to collect it, and was warned numerous times by the relative, and later on her son, to be careful as it was 'really sharp!'. As soon as I saw the edge of it, I knew it was blunt as hell. It had an almost 45 degree edge.....I ran it over my palm, as if to slice it. Not a mark on my hand. 
     A few days later the Son mentioned it again, stating that it was a sharp, dangerous weapon. I didn't say anything to deny it, what's  the point.
        A week later,  I was cutting a package open with my Cold Steel Recon 1, which had an edge that varied between 'moderately sharp' and 'so sharp that it makes me slightly scared' depending on how recently I sharpened it. I cut my package open, put the open knife on my bed, emptied the box, and then reached out to close my knife. Unfortunately my little finger brushed very lightly across the edge, and sliced off a perfect circle of skin the size of a match head. Didn't even hurt at first. Bled like a pig 😄
      Now I think about the damage that knife could do if struck hard against an arm. Easily cut to the bone. Imagine a sword that sharp? Arm gone, I think. My family have probably never encountered a truly sharp knife.

author: @MidwestArtMan
text:
*Don't try this at home disclaimer

I saw a YouTube comment or two from people in Malaysia and similar places where machetes are the weapon of choice for criminals and guns are banned. They said that chairs are a surprisingly good defensive weapon against them. You have the seat as some amount of shield, and the four legs can be used to thrust at the attacker. They're long enough to keep the machete pretty far away.

author: @MFBoom101
text:
Great Tactic for Running Away is...
Pick up random stuff & throw it at them & they'll try & block it. Even when someone throws a pillow at you your natural reaction is to put up your hands & try to stop it from hitting you in the face. 
Throwing anything at someone causes them to slow down & try to block it, which slows them down more than you, which allows you to run away faster!

text: As far as someone grabbing the blade. In Marshall arts your almost never talked to stop a blow cold it's more about redirecting and parrying. If the blade was not razor sharp I doubt someone would be able to keep the point on target when the other person is pushing it aside. That being said good luck grabbing the blade of someone who is trained. As far as razor sharp versus dangerously sharp all razor sharp edges are dangerously sharp not all dangerously sharp edges are razor sharp. It really comes down to whether or not the blade is able to shave hair if it can it is razor sharp.

author: @daymonclayton6388
text: I’m in law enforcement and we train with shock knifes and guns with simunition rounds and uncooperative but heavily padded opponents. Even with the best techniques you’re going to get cut and gun disarms are going to get you shot most of the time. And the vast majority of self defense techniques only work against cooperative opponents. Great video! I understand wanting to have something to give you a small chance vs no chance, but for decision making purposes, it’s important to understand that there is a very low probability of success. I’m not just American but a Texan so you obviously know the plan A for me and even my non law enforcement family 😂. For the rest of the world, you have my sympathy especially in places that even restrict knives,swords, pepper spray or anything to defend yourself 😢

author: @awfuldodge8772
text:
First rule of defence - don't be there.

Second rule - try not to be there.

Third rule - don't get hit.

Fourth rule - if you are going to get hit... minimise the damage taken.

Fifth rule - hyper aggression... Do massive damage first.

author: @KurNorock
text: Here is the US,  "dangerously sharp" means dull.  Dull knives are more dangerous than sharp knives because you need to use more force to cut.  More force means less control.  The overwhelming majority of people who accidentally cut themselves or others, did so with a dull blade.

replies.author: @martinoberngruber1893
replies.text:
That is the reason I sharpened the first pocket knife for my then 5yo extra sharp. So he could use it to carv wood with little force. 😊
But I think, the guys here are referring to you being on the receiving end of a blade wielded against you.😊

replies.author: @galk9767
replies.text:
Here in Sweden, I have only ever heard people refer to really sharp blades as "dangerously sharp" even by those who know that a sharp blade is a safe one.
I think "dangerously sharp" is just a way to say that a blade is really , really sharp rather than describing how dangerous it is to use. (Even if the implication is otherwise.)

replies.author: @troll_kin9456
replies.text: @galk9767 That is what it means in the US as well. The OP is being cute. No American would actually hear the term "dangerously sharp" and think the person means dull.

author: @alexh4436
text: The other point of context is your strength and conditioning.  I am now in my 50s and have spent the last 20 years working in an office.  I used to be athletic, but now I am under no illusion that I could defend myself against a younger street thug even if he only had fists, nor could I run away.  As street thugs tend to be young men, effective physical defense is not a realistic option for most, and flight is totally dependent on the thugs willingness to let you go.  That is why we need active policing and stern sentencing for criminals.  In the absence of that the bad guys rule the streets.

replies.author: @Orquet-qj2nf
replies.text: Yup. If I ever get murdered, my husband did it. Running away is not an option. I have bad knees and a tiny ribcage, he was a long distance runner in highschool.

author: @Nickle_King
text:
If you are interested in self defense, but not enough to do more than to watch a video about it, all you are is an overconfident victim-in-waiting. An assailant's favorite kind of target.

If you want to feel safe, do the work. Find a trainer. Talk to your local police about what you can get away with with the laws of your area. Do some sort of physical training. And, most important of all, know whether you have it in you to hurt someone else.

author: @arnijulian6241
text:
This is why jack chain was so popular when hackers like falchions & messers emerged from the 11th century to be fairly common in the 11 to 14th century.
If a bit of iron, steel or similar is not in the way then that limb is off.

I have jacked coaat like the jacketed jackets of the 18th & 19th century used to protect from sabres as a flat wire mesh will stop a slash or hack even if you will feel it but the torso & arms are intact.
Use to wear garrage made brigandine since 14 as after the 5th altication with a angry fella with a sharp object but brigandine or maille is not pratical in daily life.
I always carried trauma compresses/dressing since a child as a habit I picked up from from my male elders that are veterans.
I always have a dressing in my inside coat pocket of that jacketed jacket/coat I mentioned which though I have never needed a dressing personally they have come in useful for other's but better save then sorry.
I also wear a simple aluminium bowl secrete/secret/hidden helmet under my cap or a hat. 
I'm not head to toe in armour but the vitals are covered reasonably well.
Something I highly recommend is pratical footwear well fitted boots or the like as have a stable footing is a major boon.
I look at most foot wear people use & it is not suitable if anything unexpected happens.
“Luck is when preparation meets opportunity” -paraphase of Lucius Seneca.

Anyone that claims to have X technique that always works is full of nonsense.
Training in a proven combat system is of considerable value but alone without preparation & other means does not put much odds in your favour.
Still it s best to avoid conflict but sometimes you don't get a choice & so being as best prepared as you reasonably can within your means while in the confines of lawful conduct should be sort.

author: @peterprokopiuk4921
text:
The fastest way to piss off a karateka is to tell them that their 1/3/5-step sparring drills have nothing to do with self-defence. These are drills where the participants start at sword range - they were directly inspired by Kendo - and the attacker steps in to do a lunging straight punch, then freezes in place, like he forgot what he came here for, while the defender does the block and counter, as many as he likes, then reset. That same attack is often used in knife drills.

Reasons this kind of practice is dangerously wrong:

1) Tempo. It doesn't matter how skilled I am, I don't automatically get 3-5 actions per the enemy's one.

2) Action beats reaction. The defence only succeeds because the attackers lunge gives ample time to react. Those who show a knife, rarely use it, and vice versa.

3) Distance and timing. The primary excuse given for this being a "foundational" practice, ignoring the fact that both of those things are completely different in real life, just like how they are not the same in BJJ as in Boxing; it's not universal.

4) Choreography. Trying to impose order on chaos, which never ends well. Compliant practice is a necessary first step, but too often, the next steps aren't even taken.

And here's the plot twist: I've been practicing karate for 25 years.

author: @Arachnoid_of_the_underverse
text: For the time when you can't run away for whatever reason then using the outside of the forearms is a last gasp defence, as they contain less blood vessels than the inner arms and thighs.

author: @Zoddlander
text: "Dangerously sharp" or Jagged edge? Is a saw "sharper" edge than a sword? I've cute my self on a saw and they can be more devestating than a knife. But what do you think? Context matter of course.

replies.author: @somerando1073
replies.text: Against bare skin jagged edges are horrific, but saw-like blades do very poorly against sturdy clothes. Not sharp enough to directly chop through and it snags immediately so you can't do the sliding motion that enables the slice.

replies.author: @Zoddlander
replies.text: @somerando1073 I'm just wondering how a gameson will hold up against a normal saw

replies.author: @somerando1073
replies.text: @Zoddlander I think very poorly assuming a hand saw and not powered in any way.

replies.author: @uponeric36
replies.text: @Zoddlander Very good chance that someone with a decent punch would knock you out the moment the saw snags on the gambeson; especially because they're often short and not tipped so you'd have to slash. If it's a long saw they very well could just grab it and bend it out the way before beating the fuck out of you (long saws tend to be floppy) or pulling out their own pocket knife and putting it in your neck. TL;DR don't attack someone via a saw.

author: @DHBucsFan
text: 6:11 lots of talk about chopping, but always remember; "point beats edge" You can do more damage (depending on the location) with a thrust (and a good point) than with a slash or a chop. It's pricy, but I recommend the ANV Knives M500 Anthropoid - made in the Czech Republic and definitely "dangerously sharp!" 😏

author: @JamesHart62
text: Ah, the Gladiators Sword from United Cutlery! I keep mine in my bedroom, close to hand, and I've sharpened it to a 20 degree edge, and I love the little sword. But, as a leather worker who works with Leather up to 1/4th inch thick. It would cut the leather sure, but I don't think it would go through. It takes a very thin, very sharp blade to go through thick leather. If I glued two thicknesses together That would make very good armor against a blade.

author: @bradbrandon2506
text: I love how Shad is literally holding my favorite gladius. Mine actually got stolen from me. I miss that thing. It's not just a good sword, but it's also a really great tool. To add to this in response, there's a reason I prefer all my blades to have either a razors edge or a needle point. That extra sharpness REALLY matters. The only time I can easily list where it doesn't matter as much is say in the case of an axe or a hammer (obviously) because an axe can still easily kill or incapacitate with just blunt force alone, even on an armored opponent.

author: @darcraven01
text: i'd like to make a side note: the strength of the person with the blade would also be a huge factor in how much it'd be able to cut.. like if the blade is super sharp and the guy swinging it is a string bean who couldnt bust out of a wet paper bag, its going to cut you very much (some, sure.. but not too badly). whereas if the dude is like, arnold schwarzenegger in his prime.. even a dull blade is going to be dangerous just because the power they could put into the swing..

author: @MatoVuc
text:
The one universal self-defense implement i've discovered is a small, but extremely bright flashlight (mine is 1800 lumen), preferably with a clip, so you can wear it on yourself with easy access.

Even in bright daylight conditions, you flash that into someone's eyes the only thing they will see is a searing white center and everyrhing around it will be black, because the eye can't handle rhe contrast.

It is also quite uncomfortable, so the natural reaction is to duck and cover your eyes, which gives you the opportunity to exit the sotuation or fight back unfairly if you absolutely must

replies.author: @Franimus
replies.text: I face that every time I drive at night in oncoming headlights, it sucks but if I'm determined I'm plowing through it

replies.author: @MatoVuc
replies.text:
​@Franimus not the same thing, especially not at over 1500 lumens directly into the eyes.

The lights on vehicles are specifically aligned to not flash directly into your eyes.

Even if you were stupid-determined, if you can't see or are distracted and the other guy isn't, the only thing you will be plowing is the pavement.

author: @emeryquint9596
text: As a defensive tactics instructor, first thing I teach is "These are just tools on your belt, some you'll use and some you wont." We go though lots of repetition and combinations of simple moves. Granted, the training I do has more than just 'protect' but the rules are generally the same.  With a knife, you will get cut, no matter what. That's why half of the training is how to protect yourself as best as possible and then how to take the offensive, or withdraw.

author: @SahKriaann
text:
'Razor sharp' implies that it could shave your hair, because that is what razors do, they are sharp by design.
'Dangerously sharp' could mean very sharp, or very dull, based on context. In combat it means the weapon is sharp, if its in my kitchen it means that its dull as a butter knife.

author: @DavidStruveDesigns
text: Here in the UK a lot of attacks happen in one of three places: On trains (that is sadly increasingly common) where you don't really have anywhere to go, especially if it's quite crowded. On buses where again not really anywhere to go especially as more often than not you're sitting, your attacker is standing and blocking the only way to the exit. When you're in some enclosed space like a store where again they can corner you or just simply take you by surprise when your back is to them. Running away only really works if you're in the middle of a street and it's not at all crowded AND you see the person coming and realise in time they have ill intentions towards you. That's one thing these self-defence classes never seem to acknowledge: the surprise factor in most assaults with weapons. So many victims of stabbings that survived had no clue that it was about to happen, and many don't even realise they've been badly injured until moments after the attack has happened because it wasn't a sustained attack but a very fast single or double stab in less than a few seconds where at most you might think you've been punched or bumped into by accident. Clothing itself is another issue, even against relatively dull blades it's bad because clothing these days is so thin and offers zero protection - even a hoodie isn't really thick enough to stand up to most blades that will be used against you. A hoodie isn't a gambeson so it's not really comparable protection-wise, and most people I see walking around aren't exactly wearing particularly thick jackets and coats with particularly tough outer layers and thicker padding inside.

author: @jamcdonald120
text:
11:30 why yes shad, there is.  Dangerously sharp is any level of sharpness that CAN cut skin when drawn across it.  see, its dangerous.  Wouldnt give it to a child. its dangerous.  Simple

Working sharp is whatever level of sharpness that tool is at for normal use.  Probably dangerously sharp, but not necessarily.

Then there is Razor sharp where it can shave hairs and slices meat like butter.
